text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
STAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male (age 68 at time of scan) with newly diagnosed high-grade prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (4+5), PSA 87.4 ng/mL. Bone scan demonstrates extensive polyostotic skeletal metastases. CT performed for visceral staging and nodal assessment.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired with intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL, 120 mL), portal venous and delayed phase acquisition. Images reconstructed at 2.5 mm slice thickness in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Oral contrast administered. Radiation dose modulated.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, consolidation, or pleural effusion identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size criteria. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is of normal caliber with atherosclerotic mural calcification noted in the descending thoracic aorta.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No pleural thickening or effusion. Chest wall musculature intact. Osseous structures of the thorax: no definite lytic or sclerotic lesions in the visualized ribs or thoracic vertebral bodies on this examination (bone scan separately characterizes osseous disease including a left posterior 7th rib lesion; corresponding area demonstrates mild cortical irregularity here).

Thyroid: Partially visualized thyroid gland is heterogeneous with small bilateral nodules, the largest approximately 8 mm on the right, likely benign colloid nodules. Clinical correlation recommended if not previously evaluated.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with homogeneous parenchymal attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions identified. The hepatic vasculature and biliary tree are unremarkable. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.

Gallbladder and Biliary Tract: The gallbladder is present, without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated (3 mm).

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size (11.2 cm craniocaudal) with homogeneous enhancement. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: The pancreas is unremarkable in attenuation and morphology. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fat stranding.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal lesions or thickening. No adrenal masses.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis or urolithiasis. No renal masses identified. A 4 mm hypodense cortical cyst in the right upper pole, too small to characterize but favored benign. Ureters are not dilated bilaterally.

Gastrointestinal Tract: Bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or abnormal mesenteric stranding. The appendix is visualized and normal. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Retroperitoneum and Abdominal Lymph Nodes: No enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes by size criteria. Aorta atherosclerotic but not aneurysmal (maximum diameter 2.4 cm infrarenal).

---

PELVIS:

Prostate Gland: The prostate gland is markedly enlarged, estimated volume approximately 65 cc (measurement: transverse 5.8 cm x AP 4.4 cm x craniocaudal 5.1 cm). The gland demonstrates heterogeneous and irregular internal architecture with areas of low attenuation, consistent with the known adenocarcinoma. The prostatic capsule appears disrupted posterolaterally bilaterally, consistent with extracapsular extension. The seminal vesicles are enlarged and heterogeneous bilaterally with loss of the normal fat plane between the prostate and seminal vesicles, consistent with seminal vesicle invasion. No evidence of direct bladder invasion; however, the posterior bladder wall fat plane is effaced.

Bladder: The bladder is adequately distended. Bladder wall is mildly thickened posteriorly near the prostatic base, likely reactive or reflecting direct tumor proximity. No intraluminal filling defects.

Rectum and Sigmoid: Mildly diverticular sigmoid colon without acute diverticulitis. The rectal wall is not thickened. Fat stranding between the prostate and anterior rectal wall is present but discrete tissue plane maintained.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
- Right obturator lymph node: measures 2.1 cm in short axis, rounded morphology with loss of fatty hilum — enlarged and morphologically suspicious.
- Left external iliac lymph node: 1.4 cm short axis, borderline enlarged with indeterminate morphology.
- Right external iliac chain: Several nodes up to 0.9 cm, within normal size limits but borderline.
- Left obturator chain: Nodes up to 0.7 cm, within normal size limits.
- No common iliac or para-aortic lymphadenopathy identified.

Bony Pelvis (Correlate with Bone Scan): Mixed lytic and sclerotic lesions are present throughout the pelvis involving the right iliac wing and sacroiliac regions, consistent with the osseous metastatic disease characterized on bone scan. Additionally, the lumbar vertebral bodies (L2-L4) demonstrate sclerotic foci and irregular densities corresponding to the metastatic foci identified on bone scan. The right femoral head and proximal femur show subtle sclerotic changes at the level of the femoral neck, corresponding to the right femur lesion seen on bone scan.

Vasculature: The aortic bifurcation and iliac vessels are patent with atherosclerotic calcification noted in the common iliac arteries bilaterally, without hemodynamically significant stenosis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Markedly enlarged and heterogeneous prostate gland (approximately 65 cc) with imaging features consistent with the known high-grade adenocarcinoma. Extracapsular extension is present posterolaterally bilaterally. Bilateral seminal vesicle involvement with loss of the intervening fat planes.

2. Enlarged and morphologically suspicious right obturator lymph node (2.1 cm) and borderline left external iliac lymph node (1.4 cm), consistent with pelvic nodal involvement by metastatic disease.

3. Mixed lytic and sclerotic osseous lesions in the pelvis (right iliac wing, bilateral sacroiliac regions), lumbar spine (L2-L4), and proximal right femur, concordant with the extensive polyostotic skeletal metastatic disease documented on the technetium-99m bone scan.

4. No visceral metastatic disease identified. The liver, lungs, adrenal glands, and kidneys are clear without focal lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement.

5. Incidental partially characterized thyroid nodules; clinical correlation recommended if not previously assessed.

Dictated and signed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging
